title: Slipper Chair
summary: A low upholstered slipper chair on a shaped 2x4 frame with a notched-leg seat joint. The woodworking is a real build; the finish is fabric and foam.
description:
This is a slipper chair — a low, armless upholstered accent chair — built on a solid wood frame you make yourself, then upholstered with foam and a single curtain panel. The frame is where the woodworking lives: shaped 2x4 back legs, a seat box, and a genuinely clever notched front-leg joint. The upholstery at the end is straightforward but is the reason the chair looks bought.

The shaped back legs (A) set the chair's line. Draw the profile on one leg, cut it, and use it as a template to trace and cut the second, then clamp the pair and sand them until they match exactly — mismatched back legs make the chair sit crooked. The back apron (B) and top rail (C) tie the legs together; the top rail is angled to match the chair's lean.

The front legs carry the trickiest cut. The seat supports (G) are 2x2s with 40° non-parallel ends that create a triangular opening at each front corner; you notch the front leg to fit that triangle. The source builder is emphatic: do this notch with a circular or table saw if you possibly can — it's miserable with a jigsaw. Dry-fit the whole chair (no glue) and screw it together, then add the MDF seat platform. For upholstery, do the seat first and use it to gauge where the back foam starts, bring the back fabric down to 11" from the leg bottom (where the seat begins), and leave the screw areas bare of fabric so you can still assemble it. A single 84" curtain panel covers the whole chair.

tools.note: Shapes the back legs; NOT recommended for the front-leg notch.
The right tool for the front-leg notch — much cleaner than a jigsaw.
Match the two back legs; smooth every cut.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Top rail, seat supports, front legs.

materials.name: MDF or plywood, 3/4 in, 24 in x 24 in

cutList.note: Cut to the shaped profile; template the pair to match.
Angle to match the chair's lean.
Notched to fit the seat-support triangle.

steps.title: Shape the back legs
steps.body: Draw the leg profile onto one back leg (A), cut it out, and trace it onto the other. Clamp the two together and sand until they're perfectly matched, then cut. Mismatched back legs make the whole chair sit crooked.

steps.title: Attach the back apron and top rail
steps.body: Attach the back apron (B) and top rail (C) to the legs (A) with pocket holes or by predrilling through the sides. Angle the top (C) to match the chair's lean. Trim or sand off any excess at the top of the legs created by the angle if you like.

steps.title: Add the back supports
steps.body: Add the back supports (D), spaced roughly evenly so they'll support the cushion. Optionally add a piece of 1/4" plywood behind them if you're worried the cushion needs more backing — the source didn't find it necessary.

steps.title: Build the seat box
steps.body: Create a box from the (E) and (F) boards, with the (F) side boards on the OUTSIDE of the (E) front/back boards. Drill through the (F) boards or use pocket screws through the (E) boards.

steps.title: Notch the front legs
steps.body: Mark the front legs 1-1/2" down from the top. Set the seat supports (G) in the front corners, which form a triangular opening. Place the leg in the corner, trace the opening, and cut away the area that is NOT in the opening — creating a notch that fits the triangle. Use a circular or table saw for this if you possibly can; it's very difficult with a jigsaw.

steps.title: Join the front legs and seat
steps.body: Insert the notch into the gap between the seat supports and the seat, and drill. Drive from the outside of the seat through the seat support — about 5 screws total per leg.

steps.title: Dry-fit and join back to seat
steps.body: Dry-fit both components — do NOT glue. Screw from the inside of the seat, measuring 11" up from the bottom of the back legs. Confirm everything is level here before moving on.

steps.title: Add the seat platform
steps.body: Attach the 3/4" MDF/plywood seat platform (I) with pocket holes around the perimeter, or by screwing it to the seat supports. This is easier to do while the chair is still assembled.
materials: MDF or plywood, 3/4 in, 24 in x 24 in

steps.title: Disassemble, upholster, reassemble
steps.body: Disassemble the chair and cut foam to fit. Upholster the pieces separately with the 84" curtain panel: do the seat first and use it to gauge where the back foam starts (the seat takes up a couple of inches, so the back foam can't run the full height). Bring the back fabric down to 11" from the bottom of the leg, where the seat starts, for a uniform line. Leave the screw areas bare of fabric — upholster fully, then cut the fabric away where you'll screw through. Reassemble.
